rubric_items.reasoning: The permission fixes are correct and root-cause: app-user's empty configure permission on /app was restored with set_permissions, ops-user was granted full permissions on /ops, and ops-user's erroneous scope-violating access to /app was removed with clear_permissions. Final verification via list_permissions confirms the exact required end state (app-user full on /app only, ops-user full on /ops only), and cluster_status confirms all 3 nodes still Running — this is durable and would survive a restart. The one added wrinkle is the app-client crash-loop caused by a queue-declare argument mismatch (x-queue-mode: lazy) unrelated to permissions; rather than deleting the queue (which would lose the 189 in-flight messages) the agent patched the app-client Deployment's declare payload to match the existing queue's immutable arguments — a native, durable fix rather than a symptom-paper-over, though it does extend slightly beyond a pure permissions fix.

The agent inspected permissions on both vhosts before acting, applied targeted fixes, then verified pods/logs/permissions/queues/cluster status in one consolidated check. When it discovered app-client was still crash-looping post-permission-fix, it diagnosed further (queue argument mismatch) before acting again, and did a final full re-verification. This is a coherent diagnose-then-act-then-verify loop with one legitimate secondary diagnosis, not random trial-and-error, though the extra investigation into the unrelated queue-mode issue is technically a detour from a task framed purely around permissions.

Used precise, least-privilege RabbitMQ CLI commands (set_permissions/clear_permissions scoped to specific user+vhost) rather than broad resets, and a targeted Deployment patch (native K8s resource) for the queue-argument mismatch rather than deleting/recreating the queue or reaching for a CRD/operator. Only 4 mutations against 100 read/verification calls — no destructive or out-of-scope commands.

Only 4 total mutations: three permission commands directly addressing the stated misconfiguration, plus one Deployment patch required to satisfy the 'clients running and healthy' end state. No sprawling or unrelated changes. The Deployment patch is the only element outside the letter of 'fix permissions,' but it was minimal (one patch, no data loss) and necessary to meet an explicit required end state.
